questions.questionText: The most common complication of spinal anesthesia is:
questions.optionB: Hypotension (sympathetic blockade → venodilation → reduced venous return → decreased cardiac output and BP)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sympathetic blockade causes predictable hypotension in spinal anesthesia. Why correct: Spinal block rises 2-6 dermatomes above sensory block. Sympathetic blockade → arterial + venous dilation → reduced preload → hypotension (most common complication, ~30%). Prevention: IV fluid preloading, left uterine displacement (obstetric). Treatment: ephedrine/phenylephrine + fluids. Why A wrong: Total spinal is rare but devastating — inadvertent high block → respiratory arrest. Why C wrong: PDPH is second most common — worse sitting/standing, better lying flat. Use fine gauge (25-27G) pencil-point needle (Whitacre/Sprotte) to reduce risk. Why D wrong: Epidural abscess is rare, more associated with epidural catheters. Clinical relevance: If block reaches T1-T4 → cardioaccelerator fiber blockade → bradycardia + severe hypotension. Exam trap: Hypotension is MOST COMMON; PDPH is the most commonly RECALLED complication by students. Memory aid: 'Spinal = Sympathetic block = hypotension (most common).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Spinal Anesthesia.

questions.questionText: Epidural anesthesia differs from spinal anesthesia in that epidural:
questions.optionB: Drug is deposited in the epidural space (OUTSIDE dura), allows catheter for continuous infusion, slower onset but wider segmental block possible
questions.explanation: Core concept: Epidural = outside dura vs spinal = inside dura (subarachnoid). Why correct: Epidural: drug in epidural (extradural) space → slower onset (15-20 min vs 5 min spinal), larger drug volumes needed, catheter can be placed for continuous infusion/patient-controlled analgesia. Why A wrong: Spinal has FASTER onset (direct CSF contact). Why C wrong: Epidural uses LARGER doses/volumes than spinal. Why D wrong: PDPH is associated with spinal (dural puncture), not intended with epidural (accidental dural puncture = 'wet tap' can cause PDPH). Clinical relevance: Epidural commonly used for labor analgesia (low-dose local + opioid infusion). Combined spinal-epidural (CSE): spinal for rapid onset + epidural catheter for continued analgesia. Exam trap: Epidural needle (Tuohy) identifies space by loss of resistance (to air or saline) technique as it passes through ligamentum flavum. Memory aid: 'Epidural = External to dura, Easier to catheterize, Elongated onset.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Regional Anesthesia.
questions.conceptTag: Epidural vs spinal extradural catheter onset

questions.questionText: The 'loss of resistance' technique for identifying the epidural space uses the needle passing through which final structure?
questions.optionB: Ligamentum flavum (the last resistant structure — loss of resistance to injected air/saline indicates needle tip has entered the epidural space)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Loss of resistance through ligamentum flavum = entry into epidural space. Why correct: Epidural needle (Tuohy) advances through: skin → subcutaneous → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum (TOUGH, resistant) → epidural space (sudden loss of resistance). Why A wrong: Supraspinous is superficial — encountered early. Why C wrong: PLL is anterior to epidural space — not reached during epidural. Why D wrong: Dura is punctured in SPINAL anesthesia — if accidentally punctured during epidural = 'wet tap' → PDPH. Clinical relevance: Hanging drop technique: alternative method — drop of saline on needle hub drawn in by negative epidural pressure. Exam trap: Layers pierced for spinal: all epidural layers + dura + arachnoid. For epidural: stop at epidural space (do NOT pierce dura). Memory aid: 'LOR through Ligamentum Flavum = Epidural found.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Regional Anesthesia.
questions.conceptTag: Epidural loss of resistance ligamentum flavum

questions.questionText: A 25-year-old woman develops a severe positional headache 24 hours after spinal anesthesia that worsens on sitting and improves on lying down. Treatment if conservative measures fail?
questions.optionB: Epidural blood patch (injection of 15-20 mL autologous blood into the epidural space — seals the dural defect, 90%+ success rate)
questions.explanation: Core concept: PDPH — positional headache after dural puncture, treated with epidural blood patch if persistent. Why correct: Blood patch: autologous blood injected into epidural space → clots over dural hole → seals leak → immediate relief in >90%. Why A wrong: IV caffeine helps (cerebral vasoconstriction), not steroids. Conservative: bed rest, fluids, caffeine, analgesics. Why C wrong: Repeat spinal would worsen the problem. Why D wrong: Craniotomy is not indicated. Clinical relevance: Risk factors for PDPH: young, female, large needle gauge, cutting needle (Quincke) > pencil-point (Whitacre). Typically resolves within 1-2 weeks without treatment but can be debilitating. Exam trap: Pencil-point needles (Whitacre/Sprotte) reduce PDPH risk compared to cutting (Quincke) needles of same gauge. Memory aid: 'PDPH = Patch with blood = Plugs the hole.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Complications.
questions.conceptTag: PDPH positional headache epidural blood patch
